utterances: But, I think that these are - generally speaking, they have a good deal of humor to them and they also are markers of our society and how we interrelate.
So, for the archaeologists of the future, looking at this language will be a very good way of measuring who we were and what we thought was important.
How did you decide how to collect - which were the best ones?
How could you wade through all of them?
I haven't ever collected in any organized fashion to choose the best words of the year.
I've always, over the course of the year, run usually around four terms in each issue of Wired.
So, I'm really on the frontline.
I'm reading all the newspapers that I can, all the magazines that I can, looking, using Wikipedia, Google and so forth and just finding terms that seem to resonate.
And I leave it to others to decide which are the best.
And I leave it to the world at large to decide which one's will last.
This is Talk of the Nation, Science Friday from NPR News.
I'm Ira Flatow.
We're talking with Jonathon Keats, who writes the ""Jargon Watch"" column for Wired magazine.
He has a new book coming out in February called ""The Book of the Unknown."" 1-800-989-8255.
Lots of people with suggestions.
Let's go to McCloud in Santa Barbara.
Hi.
Hello.
My suggestion is the word tubed.
This is funny.
I was sitting with a friend of mine and I said, yeah, you know, that would be really good if you posted that and he just said tubed.
And without us communicating beyond that, I understood that it meant he just shoved all that information down the magic tubes onto the Internet.
And it was funny, I was just sort of listening to this and I heard somebody else say it, and I was like, oh.
Here I understood a word without any - without hearing it before.
I understood it in context.
But, you know, in a different generation that would mean a television.
He's watching the tube.
It would.
And at this point, I think it's partially pulling on the humor of the idea of the Internet as tubes, which is it isn't.
(SOUNDBITE OF LAUGHTER)
And secondarily because of YouTube and...
Right.
...that sort of thing.
You know, I hear people also do the same thing when their, you know, boyfriend's are playing on the iPhone and somebody - oh, God, he's been iPhoned.
You know, he's...
Google.
Google started it.
To google something created a verb.
Jonathon, comment?
Yes.
It can be a really powerful phenomenon when a company gains market share - Twitter being one, Google being the best example of all - such that the word comes into general parlance.
Kleenex has this, Xerox had this in the past.
And there's a term for this phenomenon and for the possibility of losing trademark control over your brand name, which is genericide.

And Google initially seems to have really supported the use of Google with a small 'g' as a term that really popularized the search engine, but after some time, came to worry a bit that this might make the term so widespread and so generic that it would no longer have the trademark protection that they initially had gotten for it and that they depend upon for their business model.
Any words that you particularly like?
I got about a minute to go.
Anything you want to throw in?
I like deep carbon.
I like these words that have deeper meanings to them in terms of bringing us to an awareness of what we might not know much about.

Deep carbon is the greenhouse gas that is locked up in the permafrost that, as global warming is exacerbated, will ultimately possibly cause much greater damage than any of the environmental factors that we typically think of.
So, we might be seeing more of these environmental words entering the jargon?
Yes.
I've been marking many of them, a lot of them to do with alternate fuels - mycodiesel, which is made with a fungus; green crude, which is made with genetically engineered algae.
So, these terms to me are fascinating because they express our concerns and they can also make people more concerned about one of the most fundamental problems facing our world today.
All right.
Dr. - Jonathan Keats, thank you for taking time to be with us.
Thank you very much.
Happy holiday - happy New Year to you.
You as well.
Jonathon Keats writes the ""Jargon Watch"" column for Wired magazine and he has a new book coming out in February called ""The Book of the Unknown."" He's also a conceptual artist.
Among his recent works, the Atheon Project at the Judah L Magnes Museum in Berkeley, California.
That's about all the time we have today.
From the studios of NPR West, this is DAY TO DAY.
I'm Madeleine Brand.
And I'm Alex Chadwick.
Coming up, the newspaper columnist who, three years ago, unmasked a CIA agent speaks at last and reveals what?
But first, seven Israeli soldiers were killed today as they patrolled the border with Lebanon.
Two others were kidnapped.
Israeli Prime Minister Ehud Olmert called the attack an act of war.
And Israel has responded, bombing Lebanese targets.
I'm joined now by Nicholas Blanford, a correspondent for The Christian Science Monitor.
He is on the Israel-Lebanon border.
And Nicholas, what do you see right now?
This is an area where Hezbollah and the Israelis have clashed quite often in the past.
And at the moment, they're being shelled quite heavily by the Israelis.
There's been a continuous shelling all afternoon, plus an air strike quite close to here.
And I think what I've been watching is fairly typical of what's been happening all along the border, which is about 70 miles long altogether.
Sure, you find him to be what, then - a great salesman, a great visionary?
What was he that was positive?

Well, we want, of course, a government of national unity because the problem that Iraq faces right now is the threat of sectarianism that the terrorists are trying to both promote and exploit, the terrorists who want to see a sectarian civil war.

And the right answer to that is to bring a government of national unity, separate the Sunni population from the terrorists, and bringing them into the government proportional to how well they did in the last election.
This is what the Shiites also say they want, but there is, of course, the issue of who should be the Prime Minister and who should be interior and defense ministers.
We have said that the defense and interior and intelligence people ought to be those that have broad support so that the institution is built in a way that has the confidence of all Iraqis.
Otherwise, it simply will not work and will play into the hands of the terrorists and insurgents.
Well, from all that we read here about your efforts, you are a very active, tough salesman, but it's a very hard sell, is the impression we get here.
Of course, the Iraqis are going through a difficult period, and there is a loss of confidence between the various communities.
And so to bring them together to accept compromises that are necessary to be made is not easy.

But I'm encouraged that increasingly they recognize that their country is bleeding and that they must come together and in the last two to three days there have been very positive and encouraging talks, very frank talks, direct talks.
So there is progress, but still, very difficult days are ahead in terms of decisions with Iraq to who will be Prime Minister and who will be the key ministers in the new government.
You assume, though, it'll be a different Prime Minister?
Have you heard that much progress?
Well, that is the attitude of some key players in Baghdad.
But I don't want to pronounce myself on this issue.
It's up to the Iraqi's to decide who their Prime Minister will be.

But you're hearing from key players whom you've been consulting with that they're taking on your point that Prime Minister Jafari is perhaps not the right guy to be running the government at this point?
I think there is broad opposition to him in Iraq, but some forces continue to support them.

And I believe that the judgment ultimately will be made whether he can unify Iraq and whether he has the record in terms of what he has done that can be an effective Prime Minister in terms of dealing with the problems that Iraq faces.

Realistically, how many months or years away do you think Iraq is from a situation in which there is a trusted national security force, so much so that a local militia cannot hang people from lampposts in Sadr City, but rather the government is the law and the power behind the law?
How far away are we from that situation?
Oh, I think we are some distance from that, but the key issue now, and one that will have a determining factor on the future of the country, is the formation of this government.

If the Iraqis get the right government, a government of national unity, with professional, broadly acceptable people running security institutions, a very, very important step would be taken in the right direction.
But you seem to be, though, in a race against time here.
That at some point, I mean, people need protection in their cities.
They have to have security.
How long do you have before people feel the government stands for that?

I think that we're talking about weeks before such a government is formed, but it is so important since this will shape the next four years of Iraq, that it is the right government and that key institutions are in the hands of people who are both competent and are broadly trusted.
Tomorrow, for Parliament's first meeting, there has been declared a no- car day in Iraq.
That is in order for Parliament to meet in security, nobody's driving in the city.
If that's a measure of how secure things are in the capital, it sounds that it's pretty perilous still.

Baghdad in particular has been the scene of the most violence in the last several days, and therefore the government has decided since the terrorists use cars for exploding in populated areas to ban the driving of cars tomorrow.
And they're likely to do that also on the 40th day of Assura.
It has proven to be a positive step in terms of reducing violence when they have done that, so they are planning to do that tomorrow.
Well Ambassador Khalilzad, thank you very much for talking with us.
I hope you'll be back on the program soon.
Well it's great to be with you.
That's Zalmay Khalilzad, the U.S.
Ambassador to Iraq, speaking to us from Baghdad.
The Muslim Brotherhood is also outlawed in Syria.
The group's leader lives in exile, and he's growing more confident he might be going home soon, courtesy of a growing political crisis in his homeland.
Syria's government is cooperating with a United Nations investigation into the murder of Rafiq Hariri, Lebanon's former prime minister.
Syria's president, Bashar Al-Assad, has denounced the probe as a US-led conspiracy, but his family's 40-year grip on power appears shakier than ever before.
NPR's Deborah Amos has our story from London.
(Soundbite of call to prayer)
A small alarm clock on a bookcase signals time for prayer for 67-year-old Ali Sadreddine Bayanouni, the exiled leader of Syria's Muslim Brotherhood.
Syria is a world away from this modest home in a London suburb.
Bayanouni stays in touch through e-mails; an Arabic-language satellite channel brings the latest news.
After 39 years of exile, Bayanouni says he's closer to going home than ever before.
Convinced the Assad regime's hard-line approach to the Hariri investigation is misguided, Bayanouni says it will eventually lead the regime to collapse.
(Through Translator) By their own behavior, it seems the regime is unbalanced.
It's lost all reason.
He is preparing Syria's Muslim Brotherhood for a political role.
Bayanouni says he wants a democratic state, not a religious one.

The Muslim Brotherhood recently signed a document called the Damascus Declaration that, for the first time, unites the Muslim Brotherhood outlawed in Syria with secular and minority opposition groups inside the country.
